sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 52, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After SPACE:
- Player white extension at col 44 (node 7: W, pixels=4, boundary=[[36, 44], [39, 44]])
- Player green body at cols 45-47 (node 8)
- Center blue square frame at cols 32-35 (node 5)
- Center blue center at cols 33-34 (node 9) - still inside the frame, NOT collected
- Left blue center at cols 41-42 (node 10) - this was inside the white extension before, now it's separate
Interesting! The white extension SHRANK from cols 40-44 (16 pixels) to just col 44 (4 pixels). The left blue center moved from cols 41-42 to... wait, it's still at cols 41-42 (node 10). So the white extension retracted, and the left blue center that was inside it is now exposed.
Also, the center blue center is still at cols 33-34, inside the frame. It was NOT collected.
So SPACE didn't work as expected. The white extension retracted instead of extending. This is different from before.
Wait, let me re-examine. Before SPACE:
- White at cols 40-44 (16 pixels)
- Green at cols 45-47
After SPACE:
- White at col 44 (4 pixels)
- Green at cols 45-47
So the white extension retracted from cols 40-44 to just col 44. This is the opposite of extending.
Hmm, maybe SPACE toggles the white extension. When the white extension is large (16 pixels), SPACE retracts it to small (4 pixels). When the white extension is small (4 pixels), SPACE extends it.
Let me try pressing SPACE again to see if it extends.
But wait, I need to understand the mechanic better. Let me look at the earlier SPACE presses:
1. At the right blue square: white was at col 43 (4 pixels), SPACE extended it to cols 43-47 (16 pixels). This collected the right blue center.
2. At the left blue square: white was at col 20 (4 pixels), SPACE extended it to cols 16-20 (16 pixels). This collected the left blue center.
3. Now at the center blue square: white was at cols 40-44 (16 pixels), SPACE retracted it to col 44 (4 pixels). This did NOT collect the center blue center.
So it seems like SPACE toggles: small (4 pixels) -> large (16 pixels) -> small (4 pixels).
The key insight is that SPACE extends the white part when it's small, and retracts it when it's large. The extension/retraction happens in the direction the white part is pointing (the side where it's attached to the green body).
So to collect the center blue center, I need:
1. White extension to be small (4 pixels)
2. White extension pointing toward the center blue square frame
3. Press SPACE to extend the white part toward the frame
Currently:
- White at col 44 (4 pixels) - small, good
- Green at cols 45-47
- White is on the LEFT of the green body, pointing LEFT
- Center blue square frame at cols 32-35, which is to the LEFT of the player
So the white extension is pointing toward the frame! Let me press SPACE again to extend it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White at col 44 (4 pixels), green at cols 45-47
# White pointing LEFT, center blue square frame at cols 32-35 (to the LEFT)
# Press SPACE to extend white toward the frame

result = action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Score:", result.get('score'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
